0: Beneficial effect of compost utilization on reducing greenhouse gas emissions in a rice cultivation system through the overall management chain
Seung Tak Jeong a Gil Won Kim a Hyun Young Hwang a Pil Joo Kim a,b, , Sang Yoon Kim C,*

<list>a Division of Applied Life Science (BK 21 + Program), Gyeongsang National University, Jinju 52828, Republic of Korea 
b Institute of Agriculture and Life Sciences, Gyeongsang National University, Jinju 52828, Republic of Korea 
C National Academy of Agricultural Science, Rural Development Administration, Wanju 55365, Republic of Korea</list>
1:
<list>Manure composting and its land appli- cation reduced net GWP by 25%. 
Impact of greenhouse gas emission from composting process was negligible. 
Compost application suppressed CH4 flux by 58% during rice cultivation.</list>
Article history: Received 19 June 2017 Received in revised form 1 September 2017 Accepted 1 September 2017 Available online 12 September 2017
Keywords: Composting Methane Nitrous oxide Life cycle assessment Global warming potential
2: Livestock manure application can stimulate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, especially methane (CH4) in rice paddy. The stabilized organic matter (OM) is recommended to suppress CH4 emission without counting the ad- ditional GHG emission during the composting process. To evaluate the effect of compost utilization on the net global warming potential (GWP) of a rice cropping system, the fluxes of GHGs from composting to land applica- tion were calculated by a life cycle assessment (LCA) method. The model framework was composed of GHG fluxes from industrial activities and biogenic GHG fluxes from the composting and rice cultivation processes. Fresh manure emitted 30 Mg CO2-eq. ha 90% and 10% of which were contributed by CH4 and nitrous oxide (N20) fluxes, respectively, during rice cultivation. Compost utilization decreased net GWP by 25% over that of the fresh manure during the whole process. The composting process increased the GWP of the industrial process- es by 35%, but the 60% reduction in CH4 emissions from the rice paddy mainly influenced the reduction of GWP during the overall process. Therefore, compost application could be a good management strategy to reduce GHG emissions from rice paddy systems.
3: Rice (Oryza sativa L.) paddies are considered one of the major meth- ane (CH4) sources. Methane has 28 times higher global warming poten- tial (GWP) than that of carbon dioxide (CO2) over a 100-year time

horizon (Pachauri et al. 2014). Agriculture sector contributes approxi- mately half (50.6%) of the anthropogenic CH4 emissions at the global level (Karakurt et al. 2012) out of which rice paddy fields contribute about 20% (Ke et al. 2014). To satisfy the demand of an increasing pop- ulation, rice production should be doubled by the year 2020, which could increase CH4 emissions by up to 50% over the present CH4 emis- sions from arable lands (Bouwman 1991).

Organic amendments such as livestock manures and crop residues have been applied worldwide to improve soil fertility and thus increase crop productivity. However, this process can increase CH4 emissions under flooded paddy soil conditions (Ma et al. 2010; Kim et al. 2012; Kim et al. 2014a & b; Hwang et al. 2017). The release of labile organic carbon (OC) compounds from decaying soil organic matter (SOM) may be an important factor for determining methanogen activity and CH4 production rates (Lu et al. 2000; Kim et al. 2012). Therefore, the ap- plication of comparatively stabilized organic substrates such as compost and biochar may be better options to mitigate CH4 emissions during rice cultivation (Zhang et al. 2010). In our previous studies (Kim et al. 2014a; Pramanik and Kim 2014), the application of composted manure de- creased CH4 emissions by approximate 20-50% during rice cultivation compared to the fresh manure treatment. The application of composted rice straw was also less effective at increasing CH4 emissions than fresh rice straw during rice cultivation (Yagi and Minami 1990; Corton et al. 2000).

However, since higher amounts of GHGs such as CO2, CH4 and ni- trous oxide (N20) could be emitted during the composting process (Fukumoto et al. 2003; Szanto et al. 2007; Ahn et al. 2011; Zhong et al. 2013), the effect of compost selection on suppressing GHG emissions during rice cultivation is still under debate. To evaluate properly the ef- fect of compost utilization on suppressing GHG emissions from a rice paddy soil system, the overall influence of composted OM application on the total GWP and GHG intensity should be evaluated by including the GHG emissions that occur during the composting process. There- fore, we assessed the effect of compost utilization (from livestock ma- nure composting to land application) on reducing GHG emissions from a rice paddy system using a life cycle assessment (LCA).

6: 2.1. Life cycle assessment (LCA) of manure composting and land application

To compare the effect of compost application on reducing GHG emis- sion, the compost treatment was selected as the main treatment. In ad- dition, no-manure and fresh manure treatments were included for comparison. Fluxes of GHGs from manure management to land applica- tion were evaluated by the LCA method using the Gabi® education soft- ware (PE International, Stuttgart, Germany) and by direct measurement.

The model framework was composed of two sources of data related to GHG emissions (Fig. 1). The first one is GHG (CH4, N20 and fossil CO2) fluxes from industrial activities, which included transportation, machin- ery operation, fertilizer production, and diesel supply processes. These GHG fluxes were based on European Emission Standards 3 and 4 (European Parliament Council 2000). The other source is biogenic GHG fluxes from manure composting (CH4, CO2 and N20) and rice cul- tivation (CH4 and N20) processes, and these direct GHG emissions were determined by on-site observation.
7: In this study, we selected a representative swine manure composting plant in Jinju, South Korea as a model from which to calcu- late the GHG emissions from a composting operation. The swine ma- nure composting plant has an area of 5.96 ha and produces approximately 5.1 thousand tons of compost (45% moisture content) annually using an agitated bay composting system. The composting
Fig. 1. Model framework of the system in composted manure treatment.
plant has two agitation lines, and each line is 80 m in length and 10 m in width. The agitated bay composting was subjected to a thermophilic stage for 3 weeks and a maturation stage for 4 weeks.

In this composting plant, swine manure was collected from the swine farm nearby. A dump truck (170 kw, 62 kgm), a front loader (349 kw, 1800 rpm) and a turner (50 kw) were used to transfer the ma- nures, to build up compost piles, which are mixed with the fresh ma- nure and the dried manure at a ratio of 8:5 (w and to turn the piles once a day during the thermophilic stage. After the thermophilic stage, the mixtures were stacked in the open-backyard for post- maturity.

9: To evaluate the GHG fluxes from compost piles, the raw materials were collected from the same swine farm and transported for composting to the experimental station at Gyeongsang National Univer- sity. In the selected farm, any bedding material was not used, and the solid and liquid phases of manure were separated by a solid-liquid sep- aration system. The initial moisture content and C/N ratio of the collect- ed solid manure were 77.6% wt-1) and 14.1, respectively (Table 1). The manure was dried for a day in a greenhouse and stabilized at an ap- proximate 70% (wt wt-1 moisture content.

The composting experiment was conducted using a conventional static composting chamber method for 45 days in a greenhouse. Six hundred and thirty-three kg of the air-dried manure was filled into a plastic box 1.2 m³ in size (1.2 m X 1.0 X 1.0 m), which was covered with expanded polystyrene (5 cm thick) to prevent heat loss. The active phase of composting was considered to be completed when the pile
10: Chemical properties of fresh and composted swine manures used for composting and field test.

Parameters|Swine manure|Swine manure Parameters|Fresh|Composted pH (1:5 with H20)|7.1 I 0.0|7.2 + 0.1 EC (ds m-1)|2.8 + 0.1|8.0 + 0.2 Total carbon (g C kg-1)|413 + 7.6|355 + 8.9 Total nitrogen (g N kg-1|29 + 0.2|114 + 4.5 C/N ratio|14.1 + 1.2|3.1 + 0.7 Dissolved organic carbon (g C kg - 1)|11.3 + 0.4|8.2 + 1.2 Dissolved organic nitrogen kg-1)|3.1 + 0.13|3.4 + 0.15

temperature stabilized at close to ambient temperature. The internal temperature of the pile was measured every day using a thermometer inserted into 3 sites in the middle part of the pile. Moisture contents of the composts in the piles were measured during the active phases of composting by choosing typical samples from the three sites at the middle depth of the pile (40-50 cm). The pile was turned over 2 times (14th and 28th days) during the composting process. During the composting process, the changes of pile height (m) were continually re- corded in order to estimate the mineralization rates of the piles.

The samples were taken using a sampling core (diameter 5 cm height 5 cm) at three different points (40-50 cm depth) during composting. The core samples were used for determining moisture con- tent and bulk density. The dried compost samples were ground for the analysis of chemical properties: pH and electrical conductivity (EC) (1:10 wt v-1, sample: water); total N content by Kjeldahl digestion; and total C content by the Tyurin method (Allison 1965).

Three biogenic GHGs (CO2, CH4 and N20) were selected as the target GHGs, and the closed chamber method was used to monitor their fluxes during the composting process; the method utilized was similar to Hutchinson and Mosier (1981). Opaque acrylic chambers (D. 24 cm and H. cm) were installed inside of the compost pile in triplicate to collect gases during the composting. The bottom of the chamber was inserted into the compost pile to a depth of 15 cm. The chambers were kept open in the pile during the monitoring period except at the times of sampling. A circulating fan and a thermometer were installed inside the chamber for mixing the gases and monitoring changes of temperature during the sampling period. Sixty mL gas-tight syringes were used to collect gas samples at 0 and 30 min after the chamber clo- sure began. Gases were sampled once a day at 10:00-11:00 am throughout the composting period. Gas samples were immediately transferred into air-evacuated glass vials (20 ml) sealed by a butyl rub- ber septum.

13: To determine the effect of compost application on GHG emissions during rice cultivation, experimental plots were installed in a typical rice paddy in the agricultural farm of Gyeongsang National University, South Korea (35° 06' N and 128° 07' E). The soil was classified as poorly drained and silty clay loam. The OM content of the soil before the exper- iment was 19.8 + 2.4 g kg and the pH was 6.16 + 0.05 (1:5 with H20). Three different treatments (control and the fresh and composted swine manures) were arrayed using a split-plot design with three rep- licates. A day before the transplanting, the same amounts (5 Mg ha-¹ on dry weight) of fresh and composted manures were applied in two manure treatments (Table 1), but manure was not added in the control treatment. In all treatments, the same levels of chemical fertilizers were applied at the recommendation rates (N-P2O5-K2O = 90-45- 57 kg ha-1 for Korean paddy fields in rice cultivation (RDA, 2010). Twenty-day-old rice (Dongjin cultivar) seedlings were transplanted on June 15, 2013. The soil was flooded with 5-6 cm depth throughout this experiment, and the water was drained for harvesting at the 100th day after transplanting. The rice was harvested manually at the 120th day after transplanting. Rice yield properties were investigated following the Korean standard method (RDA, 1995).

The closed chamber method was selected to determine two biogenic GHG (CH4 and N20) emission rates and their total fluxes during the whole rice cultivation (Rolston 1986; Ali et al. 2009; Kim et al. 2013; Kim et al. 2016). Colorless and clear acrylic chambers (L. 62 cm X W. 62 cm and H. 110 cm) were placed after rice transplanting to collect gas samples. Eight rice plants were included within the chamber. Two holes were installed in the bottom of the chambers for maintaining the water level. Chambers were kept open throughout the whole rice cropping season except at the gas collection times. A circulating fan and a thermometer were installed inside each chamber for gas mixing and temperature monitoring during the sampling time.

Because several thousand gas samples were going to be analyzed during the rice cultivation, we determined the optimum closing time of the chamber and the best sampling hour during the protocol develop- ment stage. Gas samples were collected at 0, 15, 30, and 60 min after the closing of the top of the chamber. We found a linear correlation between the closure time and GHG concentration (p<0.001) and determined the optimum closing time to be 30 min. For this reason, gas samples were collected at 0 and 30 min after the chamber closure began. In addition, the diurnal changes of GHG emissions were checked at the maximum tillering stage (20 DAT), where gas samples were collected twelve times a day, with a 2-h interval between each sampling. However, the average emission values of two different GHGs during the course of a day were not observed at the same time range. The average emission rates of CH4 and N20 were observed at approximately 8:00-10:00 and 10:00-12:00, respectively, regardless of treatments. Therefore, we col- lected gas samples three times in a day (8:00-12:00-16:00) instead of one-time sampling event to enhance the accuracy of our data and to minimize the sampling error (Kim et al. 2014a).

16: The concentrations of three biogenic GHGs (CO2, CH4 and N20) were quantified using Gas Chromatography (Shimadzu, GC-2010, Tokyo). A flame ionization detector (FID) with a methanizer and a 63 Ni electron capture detector (ECD) were used to determine CO2 and CH4, and N20 concentrations, respectively.

Gas emission rates were calculated from the increase in each gas concentration per unit surface area of the chamber over a specific time interval. The following closed-chamber equation was used for estimat- ing fluxes of the three gas species from the composting process and rice cultivation (Rolston 1986):

where F: the gas flux (mg -2h-1) p: the gas density under a standardized state, /t: the rate of gas accumulation in the chamber, V: the headspace volume (m³ of the chamber, A: the chamber cross sectional area (m²), and T (K): the absolute temperature (273 + mean temperature inner the chamber).

S.T. Jeong et al. / Science of the Total Environment 613-614 (2018) 115-122
Fig. 2. Changes of chemical and physical properties during swine manure composting. Vertical bars indicate standard deviations (n =3).

approximately 20 days and then decreased, which implied that the la- bile OM of swine manure was nearly decomposed. In the composting process, the manure pH slightly decreased with composting below 7.0 at the initial stage, but thereafter, increased to approximately 7.2. The EC of the initial piles was very low with approximately 2 ds but significantly increased with composting. It reached a value of ap- proximately 8 ds m 1 on the 16th day of composting, and thereafter, did not change significantly. Total OC concentration decreased continu- ously during the composting process. In contrast, the total N concentra- tion increased with the composting time (data not shown), which resulted in a decreasing trend of C/N ratio.

The typical emission patterns of GHGs were observed during the ma- nure composting process (Fig. 3). Carbon dioxide, CH4 and N20 emis- sion rates changed similarly with temperature during the composting process. They significantly increased with the increase in pile tempera- ture and clearly decreased with the decrease in temperature. However, the N20 emission increase was slightly less than the CH4 emission in- crease. At the compost maturation stage under a 35 °C pile temperature, negligible levels of GHGs were emitted. These emissions did not signif- icantly affect the total GHG fluxes during the composting process. Total CO2, CH4 and N20 fluxes during the 45 days of composting were approx- imately 955, 2.7 and 2.0 kg Mg compost on a dry weight basis, respectively.

22: <header>3.2. Greenhouse gas emissions during the rice cropping period </header>

To evaluate the influence of composted manure application on de- creasing GHG emissions, three different treatments (control and the fresh and composted manures) were placed with three replicates, and the emission patterns of CH4 and N20 were simultaneously monitored during the rice cultivation (Fig. 4). Methane emission patterns differed markedly with organic amendments. In the control treatment, CH4 emission rates were comparatively low at the early rice growing stage,

and then significantly increased with rice plant growth and the devel- opment of reductive soil conditions. However, the CH4 emission rate significantly decreased after the rice flowering stage to the background level at the harvesting stage.

5 Mg ha 1 of fresh manure amendment on a dry weight basis signif- icantly increased CH4 emission rates, particularly during the rice vegeta- tive growth stage within 70 days after transplanting. >80% of the total CH4 fluxes occurred during this growth stage. However, the same level of composted manure application slightly increased CH4 emission rates at the early rice growing stage within approximately 30 days after transplanting. Thereafter, CH4 emission rates did not differ be- tween the control and the composted manure treatments. Compared with the seasonal CH4 flux (341 kg ha-1) of the control treatment, fresh manure application increased the seasonal CH4 flux by 180%, but composted manure application only increased the CH4 flux by 18% (Fig. 5).

During rice cultivation, very low levels of N20 were emitted. Unlike with the CH4 emission patterns, N20 emission rates were comparatively very low at the initial rice vegetative growth stage, but apparently higher N20 emissions were observed at the rice reproductive stage. The two different types of manure additions significantly increased N20 emission rates with similar patterns. The seasonal N20 flux was 4.5 kg ha in the control, and two different types of manure applica- tion increased this flux by 120-140%. However, N20 emission patterns and fluxes were not different between the two manures.
3.3. Greenhouse gas emissions through the whole management chain

The objective of this study was to evaluate the regional GHG emis- sion factors based on the combination of manure composting and land application of the composted manure with both on-site investigation and an LCA. The major output mass reference flows of each sub-
Day after composting
|Emission rate Cumulative emission 3000 2000 1000 0| 20 15 10 5 0| 20 15 10 5 0| |0 10 20 30 40
Fig. 3. Changes of greenhouse gas emission rates during swine manure composting. Vertical bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).
process during the full process are listed in Table 2 as the life-cycle inventory.

In the control treatment, in accordance with the Korean fertilization standards (RDA, 2010), only chemical fertilizers (NPK) were applied for rice cultivation. Total GHG emissions of the complete process were ap- proximately 11.1 Mg CO2-eq. ha-1 These emissions were mostly con- tributed by the land application component (97%), and in fact, the GHG emissions from the fertilizer production and transport processes were negligible. Among the GHGs, the CH4 and N20 emitted during rice cultivation contributed 86 and 11% of the total GWP, respectively. In comparison, the CO2 emissions from industrial and transport process- es contributed only 3% of the total GWP.

In the fresh manure treatment, 5 Mg ha 1 of fresh manure (dry weight basis) and chemical fertilizers were applied. Manure application increased total GWP significantly by 169% over the control. This increase was mainly caused by land application, which contributed over 99% of the total GWP. Manure application significantly increased seasonal CH4 and N20 fluxes, by 180 and 128% over the control, respectively, but the increased CH4 emission was the main contributor. The seasonal CH4 flux during rice cultivation contributed approximately 90% of the total GWP. In comparison, the influence of the GHG emissions during manure transport and application processes was negligible.
Manure composting and compost land application increased the total GWP by approximately 100% over that of the control, which was
Control
Fresh manure
Composted manure
Day after transplanting

Fig. 4. Changes of greenhouse gas emission rates in fresh and composted swine manure applied soils during rice cultivation. The arrows indicate day of fertilization. Vertical bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

mainly influenced by the CO2 emission increase during composting and the N20 emission increase during rice cultivation. Neither the in- crease of CO2 emission during transport processes nor the CH4 emission during composting and transport processes affected the total GWP in- crease significantly. The total GWP was covered by 63 and 35% of GHG emissions during the rice cultivation and composting processes, respec- tively. The composted manure application decreased total GWP by 25% over the fresh manure during rice cultivation, mainly due to reduced CH4 emissions.

In the compost treatment, total GHG emission of the whole process was approximately 22.3 Mg CO2 eq. ha-1, 52, 25 and 23% of which was contributed by CH4, N20 and CO2, respectively. The GHG emission of the whole process mainly comes from land application (63.2%) and manure composting (34.8%). Other components such as fertilizer pro- duction (1.7%), transportation (0.1%), composting operation (0.1%), and diesel supply (0.04%) did not significantly contribute to the total GWP.

In the composted manure treatment, 5 Mg ha 1 of compost on a dry weight basis was applied before rice transplanting. Since the composting efficiency of swine manure was approximately 62.5% in this study, 8.0 Mg of swine manure was required to make 5 Mg of com- post on the dry weight basis. In this process, approximately 7.8 Mg CO2- eq. of GHGs, which was composed of 4.8, 0.4 and 2.6 Mg CO2-eq. of CO2, CH4 and N20, respectively, was emitted during the composting of 8.0 Mg ha 1 (dry weight) of manure. However, this additional GHG emission was not comparable with the effect of decreased GHG emis- sion by compost application during rice cultivation.

The composted manure application decreased total GWP by 25% over the fresh manure during rice cultivation, mainly due to reduced CH4 emissions.

In the compost treatment, total GHG emission of the whole process was approximately 22.3 Mg CO2 eq. ha-1, 52, 25 and 23% of which was contributed by CH4, N20 and CO2, respectively. The GHG emission of the whole process mainly comes from land application (63.2%) and manure composting (34.8%). Other components such as fertilizer pro- duction (1.7%), transportation (0.1%), composting operation (0.1%), and diesel supply (0.04%) did not significantly contribute to the total GWP.

In the composted manure treatment, 5 Mg ha 1 of compost on a dry weight basis was applied before rice transplanting. Since the composting efficiency of swine manure was approximately 62.5% in this study, 8.0 Mg of swine manure was required to make 5 Mg of com- post on the dry weight basis. In this process, approximately 7.8 Mg CO2- eq. of GHGs, which was composed of 4.8, 0.4 and 2.6 Mg CO2-eq. of CO2, CH4 and N20, respectively, was emitted during the composting of 8.0 Mg ha 1 (dry weight) of manure. However, this additional GHG emission was not comparable with the effect of decreased GHG emis- sion by compost application during rice cultivation.

26: <header>3.2. Greenhouse gas emissions during the rice cropping period </header>

To evaluate the influence of composted manure application on de- creasing GHG emissions, three different treatments (control and the fresh and composted manures) were placed with three replicates, and the emission patterns of CH4 and N20 were simultaneously monitored during the rice cultivation (Fig. 4). Methane emission patterns differed markedly with organic amendments. In the control treatment, CH4 emission rates were comparatively low at the early rice growing stage,

and then significantly increased with rice plant growth and the devel- opment of reductive soil conditions. However, the CH4 emission rate significantly decreased after the rice flowering stage to the background level at the harvesting stage.

5 Mg ha 1 of fresh manure amendment on a dry weight basis signif- icantly increased CH4 emission rates, particularly during the rice vegeta- tive growth stage within 70 days after transplanting. >80% of the total CH4 fluxes occurred during this growth stage. However, the same level of composted manure application slightly increased CH4 emission rates at the early rice growing stage within approximately 30 days after transplanting. Thereafter, CH4 emission rates did not differ be- tween the control and the composted manure treatments. Compared with the seasonal CH4 flux (341 kg ha-1) of the control treatment, fresh manure application increased the seasonal CH4 flux by 180%, but composted manure application only increased the CH4 flux by 18% (Fig. 5).

During rice cultivation, very low levels of N20 were emitted. Unlike with the CH4 emission patterns, N20 emission rates were comparatively very low at the initial rice vegetative growth stage, but apparently higher N20 emissions were observed at the rice reproductive stage. The two different types of manure additions significantly increased N20 emission rates with similar patterns. The seasonal N20 flux was 4.5 kg ha in the control, and two different types of manure applica- tion increased this flux by 120-140%. However, N20 emission patterns and fluxes were not different between the two manures.

32: The use of livestock manures has been widely accepted as an impor- tant organic amendment that can improve soil fertility and thus substi- tute for chemical fertilizers (Bationo and Mokwunye, 1991). In this study, fresh and composted manures increased rice grain yield by 15% over the control treatment (Table 3). However, the release of soluble organic C compounds from manure amendments can increase methanogen activities and thus increase CH4 production from the irri- gated paddy soils (Ma et al. 2010; Kim et al. 2014b). We also observed that fresh manure application increased the seasonal CH4 flux by 180% over the control during the 120 days of rice cultivation (Table 2).

Methane is the metabolic end-product of strictly anaerobic methanogens (Hoehler et al. 1994; Conrad 1999; Demirel and Scherer, 2008). During rice cultivation, CH4 emission rates are comparatively low at the earlier growth stage, and then significantly increase with the development of reductive soil conditions and growing rice plants (Kim et al. 2012, 2013). The highest CH4 emissions in no OM amended soils generally occur during the initial rice reproductive stage, which may be attributed to increased availability of labile organic C in soil (Sasada et al. 2011; Kim et al. 2014a) and enhanced conductivity of
33: The use of livestock manures has been widely accepted as an impor- tant organic amendment that can improve soil fertility and thus substi- tute for chemical fertilizers (Bationo and Mokwunye, 1991). In this study, fresh and composted manures increased rice grain yield by 15% over the control treatment (Table 3). However, the release of soluble organic C compounds from manure amendments can increase methanogen activities and thus increase CH4 production from the irri- gated paddy soils (Ma et al. 2010; Kim et al. 2014b). We also observed that fresh manure application increased the seasonal CH4 flux by 180% over the control during the 120 days of rice cultivation (Table 2).

Methane is the metabolic end-product of strictly anaerobic methanogens (Hoehler et al. 1994; Conrad 1999; Demirel and Scherer, 2008). During rice cultivation, CH4 emission rates are comparatively low at the earlier growth stage, and then significantly increase with the development of reductive soil conditions and growing rice plants (Kim et al. 2012, 2013). The highest CH4 emissions in no OM amended soils generally occur during the initial rice reproductive stage, which may be attributed to increased availability of labile organic C in soil (Sasada et al. 2011; Kim et al. 2014a) and enhanced conductivity of
34: Greenhouse gas fluxes of the total processes from manure composting to land application in rice cultivation system.

CH4 through the aerenchyma channels of rice plants (Mariko et al. 1991). Unlike with the control and compost treatments, the CH4 emis- sion rates in the soils with fresh manure applied were sharply increased in the early vegetative growth stage of rice (Fig. 4); this may be caused by high availability of labile organic C released from fresh manure (Inubushi et al. 2003; Kim et al. 2014a). The addition of easily decom- posable OM such as fresh manures can stimulate the activity of soil mi- croorganisms (De Nobili et al. 2001) including cellulolytic bacteria, which may produce soluble organic C compounds and then increase the activity of methanogens (Pramanik and Kim 2014). As a result, the ease of OM decomposition in applied organic substrates could be a rate-limiting step of CH4 production (Ferry and Kastead 2007; Pramanik and Kim 2014).

Therefore, application of stabilized organic substrates, such as com- post, is strongly recommended as a practical option to suppress CH4 emission from rice cultivation fields (Chen et al. 2011; Singh et al. 2012). Compost is nutrient-rich and stabilized organic C material pro- duced by active microbial decomposition (Hoitink and Kuter, 1986). Manure composting decreased dissolved organic C content (labile or- ganic C) by approximately 70% from that of fresh manure (Table 1), and then the composted manure application increased CH4 emission by approximately 20% of over the control, but it was approximately 60% lower than that in the fresh manure application (Table 2). In our previous study, cattle manure compost application reduced seasonal CH4 flux by approximately 50% from that of the fresh manure in a tem- perate rice field (Kim et al. 2014a). Similarly, compost application changed the community structure of methanogens, and this change sig- nificantly reduced CH4 emission in an Indian rice paddy soil (Singh et al. 2012).

Manure application increased N20 fluxes by two times over those of the control, but there was no difference between the fresh and composted manures (Table 2). Since the GWP of N20 (265 times the equivalent amount of CO2) is 9 times more than that of CH4 (28 times the equivalent amount of CO2) (Pachauri et al. 2014), a small increase of N20 emission can significantly affect the net GWP. However, the sea- sonal N20 fluxes from manure amended soils were in the range from 2707 to 2873 kg CO2-eq. ha which were not comparable with the CH4 flux (26,721 and 11,239 kg CO2-eq. ha-1 in fresh and composted manure treatment, respectively). The increased N20 fluxes by manure addition amounted to only 9 and 20% of the seasonal GWP in the fresh and composted manure treatments, respectively. As a result, the composted manure application decreased seasonal GWP by 52% over that of the fresh manure treatment during rice cropping due to the re- duced CH4 emissions.

Composting is a biological decomposition process of organic mate- rials, and therefore, high emissions of biogenic GHGs such as CH4, CO2 and N20 are expected during the composting process (Hao et al. 2001). In this study, a total of 7.8 Mg CO2-eq. of GHGs, 61, 5 and 34% of which were contributed by CO2, CH4 and N20 emissions, respectively, was additionally emitted during swine manure composting to produce 5 Mg of compost (dry weight basis) (Table 2). Therefore, to determine the effect of compost application on reducing GHG emission impacts in rice fields, the overall weight of GHG emissions during the total pro- cess from manure composting to its compost land application should be systematically evaluated and should be compared with fresh manure.

Approximately 40% of the initial total C of swine manure on a dry weight basis was emitted in the form of GHG emissions, but only 0.1% of the initial total C was emitted as CH4 gas (Fig. 2). This CH4 emission was much lower than that in other manure windrow systems (Hao et al. 2004; Ahn et al. 2011). Approximately 2.7 and 2.0% of total C for straw-mixture and woodchips, respectively, were emitted as CH4 dur- ing windrow composting processes (Hao et al. 2004). In addition, only 3% of the initial total N of swine manure, which has a low C/N ratio (14.1), was emitted as N20 during the composting process (Fig. 2). It was slightly higher than 2.12 and 1.53% of total N from a long manure windrow composting and an aerated static system of sewage sludge
composting, respectively (Czepiel et al. 1996). Approximately 0.1-2.2% of total N was lost as N20 from a yard waste and sludge windrow (Barton and Atwater 2002).

In the fresh manure treatment, a total of 29.4 Mg CO2-eq. of GHGs was emitted during rice cultivation, and >98% of the GHGs came from manure land application. The influence of transport and machin- ery activities at net GWP scale was negligible in comparison. In the com- post treatment, a total of 22.3 Mg CO2-eq. ha of GHGs was emitted during the whole process of manure management. This budget was 25% lower than that in the fresh manure treatment. The net GWP was weighted by approximately 35% and 63% from the composting process and land application, respectively, while only <2% of total GHGs were contributed by industrial and transport processes. This reduction was mainly caused by reduced CH4 emissions during rice cultivation. Meth- ane has 28 times higher GWP than CO2 over a 100 year time horizon (Pachauri et al. 2014).

In the modern agricultural industry, crop yields and environment impacts might be simultaneously evaluated when determining the proper soil management strategy. More environmentally friendly agri- cultural systems should be explored with lower net GWP and GHGI and higher crop productivity for food security (Haque et al. 2016). The yield scaled GHG emissions were calculated by dividing net GWP by grain yield (Mosier et al. 2006; Qin et al. 2010; Haque et al. 2016). Live- stock manure application significantly increased rice productivity, with no difference between fresh and composted manure. However, composted manure was very effective in reducing GHG emissions from rice paddy soils. Its application reduced GHGI by approximately 26% over that of fresh manure application from the total management processes, mainly due to reduced CH4 emission from the land applica- tion component. The increase of GHG emissions from industrial and transport processes negligibly affect the total GHG emissions impact. Therefore, composting and compost application could be encouraged as a way to improve soil quality and productivity while minimizing the GHG emissions impact.

composting, respectively (Czepiel et al. 1996). Approximately 0.1-2.2% of total N was lost as N20 from a yard waste and sludge windrow (Barton and Atwater 2002).

In the fresh manure treatment, a total of 29.4 Mg CO2-eq. of GHGs was emitted during rice cultivation, and >98% of the GHGs came from manure land application. The influence of transport and machin- ery activities at net GWP scale was negligible in comparison. In the com- post treatment, a total of 22.3 Mg CO2-eq. ha of GHGs was emitted during the whole process of manure management. This budget was 25% lower than that in the fresh manure treatment. The net GWP was weighted by approximately 35% and 63% from the composting process and land application, respectively, while only <2% of total GHGs were contributed by industrial and transport processes. This reduction was mainly caused by reduced CH4 emissions during rice cultivation. Meth- ane has 28 times higher GWP than CO2 over a 100 year time horizon (Pachauri et al. 2014).

In the modern agricultural industry, crop yields and environment impacts might be simultaneously evaluated when determining the proper soil management strategy. More environmentally friendly agri- cultural systems should be explored with lower net GWP and GHGI and higher crop productivity for food security (Haque et al. 2016). The yield scaled GHG emissions were calculated by dividing net GWP by grain yield (Mosier et al. 2006; Qin et al. 2010; Haque et al. 2016). Live- stock manure application significantly increased rice productivity, with no difference between fresh and composted manure. However, composted manure was very effective in reducing GHG emissions from rice paddy soils. Its application reduced GHGI by approximately 26% over that of fresh manure application from the total management processes, mainly due to reduced CH4 emission from the land applica- tion component. The increase of GHG emissions from industrial and transport processes negligibly affect the total GHG emissions impact. Therefore, composting and compost application could be encouraged as a way to improve soil quality and productivity while minimizing the GHG emissions impact.
39: Greenhouse gas fluxes of the total processes from manure composting to land application in rice cultivation system.

CH4 through the aerenchyma channels of rice plants (Mariko et al. 1991). Unlike with the control and compost treatments, the CH4 emis- sion rates in the soils with fresh manure applied were sharply increased in the early vegetative growth stage of rice (Fig. 4); this may be caused by high availability of labile organic C released from fresh manure (Inubushi et al. 2003; Kim et al. 2014a). The addition of easily decom- posable OM such as fresh manures can stimulate the activity of soil mi- croorganisms (De Nobili et al. 2001) including cellulolytic bacteria, which may produce soluble organic C compounds and then increase the activity of methanogens (Pramanik and Kim 2014). As a result, the ease of OM decomposition in applied organic substrates could be a rate-limiting step of CH4 production (Ferry and Kastead 2007; Pramanik and Kim 2014).

Therefore, application of stabilized organic substrates, such as com- post, is strongly recommended as a practical option to suppress CH4 emission from rice cultivation fields (Chen et al. 2011; Singh et al. 2012). Compost is nutrient-rich and stabilized organic C material pro- duced by active microbial decomposition (Hoitink and Kuter, 1986). Manure composting decreased dissolved organic C content (labile or- ganic C) by approximately 70% from that of fresh manure (Table 1), and then the composted manure application increased CH4 emission by approximately 20% of over the control, but it was approximately 60% lower than that in the fresh manure application (Table 2). In our previous study, cattle manure compost application reduced seasonal CH4 flux by approximately 50% from that of the fresh manure in a tem- perate rice field (Kim et al. 2014a). Similarly, compost application changed the community structure of methanogens, and this change sig- nificantly reduced CH4 emission in an Indian rice paddy soil (Singh et al. 2012).

Manure application increased N20 fluxes by two times over those of the control, but there was no difference between the fresh and composted manures (Table 2). Since the GWP of N20 (265 times the equivalent amount of CO2) is 9 times more than that of CH4 (28 times the equivalent amount of CO2) (Pachauri et al. 2014), a small increase of N20 emission can significantly affect the net GWP. However, the sea- sonal N20 fluxes from manure amended soils were in the range from 2707 to 2873 kg CO2-eq. ha which were not comparable with the CH4 flux (26,721 and 11,239 kg CO2-eq. ha-1 in fresh and composted manure treatment, respectively). The increased N20 fluxes by manure addition amounted to only 9 and 20% of the seasonal GWP in the fresh and composted manure treatments, respectively. As a result, the composted manure application decreased seasonal GWP by 52% over that of the fresh manure treatment during rice cropping due to the re- duced CH4 emissions.

Composting is a biological decomposition process of organic mate- rials, and therefore, high emissions of biogenic GHGs such as CH4, CO2 and N20 are expected during the composting process (Hao et al. 2001). In this study, a total of 7.8 Mg CO2-eq. of GHGs, 61, 5 and 34% of which were contributed by CO2, CH4 and N20 emissions, respectively, was additionally emitted during swine manure composting to produce 5 Mg of compost (dry weight basis) (Table 2). Therefore, to determine the effect of compost application on reducing GHG emission impacts in rice fields, the overall weight of GHG emissions during the total pro- cess from manure composting to its compost land application should be systematically evaluated and should be compared with fresh manure.

Approximately 40% of the initial total C of swine manure on a dry weight basis was emitted in the form of GHG emissions, but only 0.1% of the initial total C was emitted as CH4 gas (Fig. 2). This CH4 emission was much lower than that in other manure windrow systems (Hao et al. 2004; Ahn et al. 2011). Approximately 2.7 and 2.0% of total C for straw-mixture and woodchips, respectively, were emitted as CH4 dur- ing windrow composting processes (Hao et al. 2004). composting, respectively (Czepiel et al. 1996). Approximately 0.1-2.2% of total N was lost as N20 from a yard waste and sludge windrow (Barton and Atwater 2002).

43: Livestock manure application was effective in increasing rice yields but significantly increased CH4 and N20 emissions from paddy soil dur- ing the cropping period. Manure composting and transport processes added 3 Mg CO2-eq. of GHGs for every 5 Mg of compost that was made. However, its compost application decreased 60% of the CH4 emis- sion that occurred during rice cropping, and thus reduced net GWP by 25% over that of the fresh manure through the whole management pro- cesses. The GHG emissions from composting and transport processes did not significantly affect net GWP in rice paddy conditions through the whole management processes. Compost application could, there- fore, be a good management strategy to improve rice productivity and minimize the GHG emissions increase in paddy ecosystems.
